retro_required_reason: user said 'ship it' + 2630-line diff + 3+ failed WS-protocol attempts + memory entries written

what_went_right: Code-review-then-fix gate worked as designed: the pre-tag review on commit 393b626 flagged 2 critical + 3 should-fix findings and v0.1.10 (03354eb) shipped before the v0.1.0 tag was applied — the cachedProxyTabId tab-leak loop and the unwrapComment `!raw.id` edge case would have bitten real users.

When the WebSocket protocol resisted decoding through 5 fix commits, the team made the right call in v0.1.9 to ship REST polling as the primary live-update path with WS deferred — Substack's own native client polls at ~10s anyway, so v0.1 users get a working feature instead of a broken-but-clever one.

Cross-origin cookie failure (v0.1.2) was diagnosed and replaced with a clean tab-proxy pattern (chrome.scripting.executeScript in MAIN world) that's structurally sound and self-documented in lib/api.js — not a hack pile.

Author-rendering recovery (v0.1.6 → v0.1.8) eventually built a cross-response user-table harvest that pulls user objects from top-level `users`, `post.communityPost.author`, `post.recent_commenters`, and per-reply `recent_commenters` — defensive in a way the first cut wasn't.
what_went_wrong: v0.1.0 shipped with at least three live-blocking bugs that surfaced only in user testing: (a) cross-origin cookies not attaching (v0.1.2), (b) REST `/comments` author shape didn't include user objects inline so every message rendered as 'Unknown' (v0.1.6–v0.1.8), (c) WebSocket subscribe frames rejected with 'Invalid message' (v0.1.3–v0.1.8, never fixed). Nine follow-up commits required to make the v0.1 cut actually functional.

The memory file claimed the WS protocol was 'confirmed' but the verification was passive (open socket, watch idle channel) — the actual `chat:new-comment` event flow was never exercised end-to-end during the probe phase, so the post_id-vs-postUuid type mismatch (still unfixed, deferred to v0.2) was latent in shipped code.

Pre-tag code review caught a proxy-tab leak (chrome.scripting.executeScript can throw 'No tab with id' if the tab dies between findProxyTab() and the script run, leaving cachedProxyTabId pointing at a corpse forever) — that's a single try/catch the original author missed despite owning the file. Pattern: error paths around chrome.* async APIs need explicit cache-invalidation thought, not implicit.

Repo started nested at ~/signaldeck/betterssc/ (zero semantic relationship to SignalDeck) and was moved to ~/betterssc/ mid-session via the `02ef2f2` import commit. The session memory file documenting the entire Substack DOM + REST + WS protocol now lives stranded in the wrong project memory dir (-Users-indersabharwal-signaldeck/memory/) and won't auto-load in future BetterSSC sessions.

Diagnostic console.log noise was left in shipped code — `app.js` has at least 6 `console.log('[BetterSSC ...')` statements that were instrumented during the v0.1.3-v0.1.8 debug loop and never removed. Some are useful (REST shape sample), most are not (ingest sample shape, render group count). A v0.2 prep step should strip them or gate them behind a debug flag.
three_whys: Why 1: v0.1.0 needed 9 follow-up commits (v0.1.1–v0.1.10) because three classes of bug only surfaced under real user testing — cross-origin cookie failure, missing-author render path, and broken WS subscribe.

Why 2: The pre-v0.1 probe phase verified each protocol component in isolation (REST endpoints returned 200, WS auth handshake returned OK, DOM probe found bubbles) but never ran the full integrated flow end-to-end as a logged-in user before declaring 'feasibility confirmed' and committing to v0.1.

Why 3: 'Confirmed' was conflated with 'individually responded 200' instead of 'live message round-trip observed and rendered.' The memory file documented inferred shapes (event envelope from a passive sniff, REST response shape from a public-only endpoint) as load-bearing facts. The cure is a v0.0.x integration smoke test that causes the exact events the production code path consumes (send a chat message, watch WS fire chat:new-comment, watch app.js render it) before any v0.1 ship.

evidence_citations.quote: fix(v0.1.2): proxy API via Substack tab; WS sends auth+subscribe frames

Direct fetch() from the extension page (chrome-extension://) to substack.com sends the request but does NOT attach the user's session cookie, even with `credentials: "include"` and host_permissions for substack.com. (SameSite=Lax/Strict cookies are not first-party from an extension origin.) The publication-public endpoint works without auth so it looked fine in v0.1, but the comments/inbox/realtime-token endpoints returned reduced-scope responses, causing empty chats and chat-tier WS subscription failures.

Critical — invalidate cachedProxyTabId on executeScript throw (lib/api.js): chrome.scripting.executeScript can throw "No tab with id: N" if the proxy tab closes between findProxyTab() returning and the script actually running. Previously that throw propagated unchanged and left cachedProxyTabId set to the dead id, so every subsequent API call would re-target the same dead tab and fail.

WS smoke test verified — connection opens, auth frame `{token: <JWT>}` accepted with `{"error":null,"data":{"status":"OK"}}`. After ~250ms with no second frame, server sends `{"error":"Invalid message","data":null}` — implies the protocol expects a subscribe/publish follow-up frame after auth.
feat(v0.1.9): polling fallback for live updates (WS deferred)

WS is unreliable in v0.1 (Substack's protocol rejects our subscribe frames with Invalid message for reasons we haven't pinned down). Polling matches what Substack's own native client does anyway (~10s interval with ?after=<ISO>).

Deferred to v0.2 (per review): WS post_id (int) vs state.postUuid (string) mismatch — WS already documented as broken and polling covers, but this is the first thing to fix when tackling WS protocol in v0.2.
chore: import v0.0.4 probe baseline from signaldeck/betterssc

BetterSSC = "Better Substack Chat." Chrome extension that overlays a Discord-style three-pane view on top of Substack Chat. Lives at `/Users/indersabharwal/signaldeck/betterssc/` (should be split into its own git repo — currently nested inside SignalDeck, separate product).
